news_summary: Recent advances in innovative therapies and technologies, including a sugar-coated treatment for brain cancer, testicular tissue transplants restoring fertility, AI-enhanced CRISPR tools, and affordable natural mosquito repellents, are significantly improving health outcomes and accessibility worldwide.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes en matière de thérapies et de technologies innovantes, notamment un traitement à base de sucre contre le cancer du cerveau, les greffes de tissu testiculaire permettant de restaurer la fertilité, les outils CRISPR optimisés par l'IA et les répulsifs naturels contre les moustiques à prix abordable, améliorent considérablement les résultats sanitaires et l'accessibilité aux soins dans le monde entier.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en terapias y tecnologías innovadoras —entre los que se incluyen un tratamiento recubierto de azúcar para el cáncer de cerebro, trasplantes de tejido testicular que restauran la fertilidad, herramientas CRISPR potenciadas por la inteligencia artificial y repelentes naturales de mosquitos asequibles— están mejorando significativamente los resultados sanitarios y la accesibilidad en todo el mundo.

articles.title: This sugar-coated therapy boosted survival against deadly brain cancer by 50% in mice
articles.summary: A new experimental treatment may have found a way to outsmart glioblastoma’s toughest defense: the blood-brain barrier. Researchers used sugar-coated nanoparticles to ferry genetic instructions that restore a key tumor-suppressing protein directly into brain cancer cells. In mouse studies, the therapy increased median survival by 50% while shrinking tumors without noticeable damage to other organs.
articles.content:
Researchers at Oregon State University have developed a promising experimental strategy for treating glioblastoma, the most aggressive form of brain cancer. Fewer than 30% of patients survive for two years after diagnosis.

The work, led by Oleh Taratula, Olena Taratula and Yoon Tae Goo of the OSU College of Pharmacy, focuses on two major problems that have long limited glioblastoma treatment. First, therapies must cross the blood-brain barrier, a tightly controlled network of cells that protects the central nervous system from substances circulating in the bloodstream. Second, the treatment must reach tumor cells without affecting healthy tissue.

Sugar-Coated Nanoparticles Target Brain Tumors

In a mouse model, the researchers tested lipid nanoparticles filled with genetic material designed to restore the body's ability to suppress tumor growth. The particles were then covered with a sugar coating that helped them enter the brain and concentrate inside tumors.

According to findings published in the Journal of Controlled Release, the approach increased median survival time by 50% in mice with glioblastoma.

The sugar used in the coating was mannose, which is closely related to glucose, the body's main energy source. Cells lining blood vessels in the brain contain a transporter called GLUT1 that normally carries glucose into the central nervous system. GLUT1 can also recognize mannose, allowing the coated nanoparticles to use the same pathway to cross the blood-brain barrier.

"Blood contains relatively high concentrations of glucose, and that's what the nanoparticles are competing against for GLUT1's attention," Oleh Taratula said. "For the nanoparticles to get it, they need a densely coated sugar surface, and that's our central innovation. By chemically connecting mannose to cholesterol, a major structural component of the nanoparticles, we improved surface coverage sixfold."

Delivering Tumor-Suppressing mRNA

The nanoparticles carried messenger RNA that directs cells to produce PTEN, a protein that helps prevent uncontrolled tumor growth. PTEN is often missing or inactive in glioblastoma cells.

To protect the mRNA from breaking down before it reached its target, the researchers added a positively charged cholesterol derivative that helped keep the genetic material securely enclosed inside the nanoparticles.

Glioblastoma cells also produce unusually high levels of GLUT1. That difference helped the sugar-coated particles accumulate more heavily in tumors after crossing into the brain.

"Glioblastoma is metabolically reprogrammed and expresses GLUT1 at three times the levels of normal brain tissue, so the particles preferentially accumulate in tumor tissue after crossing the blood-brain barrier," Olena Taratula said. "And restoring PTEN expression in tumor cells reinstates growth control. Across repeated dosing, tumor shrinkage occurred without any measurable organ toxicity."

A Deadly and Difficult-to-Treat Cancer

Glioblastoma affects about 3.19 people per 100,000 in the United States. It occurs more often in males than females, and the median age at diagnosis is 64. More than 95% of patients die within five years of being diagnosed.

Vincent Cataldi, Vladislav Grigoriev, Neera Yadav, Tetiana Korzun, Chao Wang and Adam Alani of the College of Pharmacy also contributed to the study.

The research was supported by the National Cancer Institute of the National Institutes of Health, the Eunice Kennedy Shriver National Institute of Child Health and Human Development, and the National Research Foundation of Korea.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant experimental breakthrough in treating glioblastoma, a deadly brain cancer with very low survival rates. The sugar-coated nanoparticle therapy successfully crosses the blood-brain barrier to deliver tumor-suppressing mRNA, increasing median survival by 50% in mice and shrinking tumors without organ damage. This represents a meaningful advance with broad potential impact on cancer treatment and patient outcomes.
articles.personality_title: Sugar-coated nanoparticles improve survival in mice with deadly brain cancer
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Glioblastoma is a very aggressive form of brain cancer with low survival rates. One major challenge in treating it is that medicines have trouble crossing the blood-brain barrier, a protective shield around the brain. Treatments also need to target cancer cells without harming healthy brain tissue.

**What happened** – Scientists at Oregon State University created tiny fat-based particles called lipid nanoparticles that carry genetic instructions to restore a protein that helps stop tumor growth. These particles are coated with a sugar called mannose, which tricks the brain’s blood vessels into letting them through. In tests with mice having glioblastoma, this therapy increased their median survival time by 50% and shrank tumors without damaging other organs.

**Impact** – This approach is unique because it uses a sugar coating to cross the blood-brain barrier and target cancer cells more effectively. The sugar coating takes advantage of a natural transporter in the brain that usually carries glucose, the brain’s energy source. Since glioblastoma cells have more of these transporters, the treatment focuses on tumors while avoiding healthy brain cells. This method shows a new way to deliver treatments directly to brain tumors.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely continue testing this therapy to confirm its safety and effectiveness in more animal studies. If successful, the next step would be to start clinical trials in humans to see if it can help patients with glioblastoma.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists developed sugar-coated nanoparticles that cross the brain’s defenses to deliver a gene therapy, boosting survival and shrinking tumors in mice with deadly brain cancer.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des nanoparticules sucrées améliorent la survie chez la souris atteinte d’un cancer du cerveau mortel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le glioblastome est une forme très agressive de cancer du cerveau avec un faible taux de survie. Un grand défi pour le traiter est que les médicaments ont du mal à traverser la barrière hémato-encéphalique, une protection autour du cerveau. Les traitements doivent aussi cibler les cellules cancéreuses sans abîmer les cellules saines.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université d’Oregon ont créé de toutes petites particules de lipides qui transportent des instructions génétiques pour restaurer une protéine aidant à stopper la croissance des tumeurs. Ces particules sont recouvertes d’un sucre appelé mannose, qui leur permet de traverser les vaisseaux sanguins du cerveau. Chez des souris atteintes de glioblastome, ce traitement a augmenté la durée médiane de survie de 50 % et réduit les tumeurs sans endommager les autres organes.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est unique car elle utilise un revêtement sucré pour franchir la barrière hémato-encéphalique et cibler plus efficacement les cellules cancéreuses. Le sucre profite d’un transporteur naturel dans le cerveau qui transporte normalement le glucose, la source d’énergie du cerveau. Comme les cellules de glioblastome ont plus de ces transporteurs, le traitement se concentre sur les tumeurs tout en épargnant les cellules saines. Cette technique ouvre une nouvelle voie pour livrer des traitements directement aux tumeurs cérébrales.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement continuer à tester ce traitement pour vérifier sa sécurité et son efficacité chez d’autres animaux. S’il réussit, la prochaine étape serait d’entamer des essais cliniques chez l’humain pour voir s’il peut aider les patients atteints de glioblastome.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont développé des nanoparticules sucrées qui traversent les défenses du cerveau pour délivrer une thérapie génique, augmentant la survie et réduisant les tumeurs chez la souris atteinte d’un cancer du cerveau mortel.
articles.personality_title_es: Nanopartículas recubiertas de azúcar mejoran la supervivencia en ratones con cáncer cerebral mortal
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El glioblastoma es una forma muy agresiva de cáncer cerebral con bajas tasas de supervivencia. Un gran reto para tratarlo es que los medicamentos tienen dificultad para cruzar la barrera hematoencefálica, una protección alrededor del cerebro. Los tratamientos también deben atacar las células cancerosas sin dañar las células sanas.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad Estatal de Oregón crearon pequeñas partículas de lípidos que llevan instrucciones genéticas para restaurar una proteína que ayuda a detener el crecimiento tumoral. Estas partículas están recubiertas con un azúcar llamado manosa, que les permite atravesar los vasos sanguíneos del cerebro. En pruebas con ratones con glioblastoma, esta terapia aumentó el tiempo medio de supervivencia en un 50 % y redujo los tumores sin dañar otros órganos.

**Impacto** – Este método es único porque usa un recubrimiento de azúcar para cruzar la barrera hematoencefálica y atacar mejor las células cancerosas. El azúcar aprovecha un transportador natural en el cerebro que normalmente lleva glucosa, la fuente de energía del cerebro. Como las células de glioblastoma tienen más de estos transportadores, el tratamiento se centra en los tumores mientras evita las células sanas. Esta técnica muestra una nueva forma de llevar tratamientos directamente a los tumores cerebrales.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente continuarán probando esta terapia para confirmar su seguridad y efectividad en más estudios con animales. Si tiene éxito, el siguiente paso sería comenzar ensayos clínicos en humanos para ver si puede ayudar a los pacientes con glioblastoma.

**Frase clave** – Científicos desarrollaron nanopartículas recubiertas de azúcar que cruzan las defensas del cerebro para entregar una terapia genética, aumentando la supervivencia y reduciendo tumores en ratones con cáncer cerebral mortal.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing brain-shaped network of delicate blood vessels, with golden sugar-coated nanoparticles shaped like tiny, shining spheres gently crossing a translucent barrier into a vibrant tumor cluster that softly pulses with renewed life, all rendered in soft natural tones of amber, cream, and muted greens.

articles.title: Man’s ability to make sperm restored after testicular tissue transplant: what scientists think
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 17 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02191-3Testicle sample removed from a ten-year-old before chemotherapy and frozen proves viable 16 years later.
articles.content:
Developing sperm cells (blue; artificially coloured) fill the centre of seminiferous tubules (orange), which form the human testis.Credit: Susumu Nishinaga/Science Photo Library

A cutting-edge operation has restored a man’s ability to make sperm by transplanting tissue samples that were removed from one of his testicles and frozen 16 years earlier, when he was still a boy. Scientists say that the remarkable achievement could mark the beginning of a new wave of fertility treatments.

The samples were taken shortly before the boy received chemotherapy treatments that put his fertility at risk. “This is an important breakthrough,” says Rod Mitchell, a paediatric endocrinologist at the University of Edinburgh, UK, who was not involved in the work. “This offers hope for prepubertal boys who are facing treatments, such as chemotherapy and radiotherapy, that can affect their future fertility.”

The work was presented at the European Society of Human Reproduction and Embryology annual meeting in London last week and was released as a unreviewed preprint1 on medRxiv earlier this year.

Looking to the future

The growing number of children who survive childhood cancer has trained the spotlight on the long-term consequences — including fertility loss — of the aggressive therapies that they receive. From 2002 to 2022, more than 3,000 boys at 16 sites in Europe, Australia and the United States opted to freeze samples from their testicles in the hope that, if they were to become infertile, the tissue could be used to restore their ability to have children2. But clinicians could not assure their young patients and their families that such a procedure would work, says Mitchell.

Lab-grown sperm: scientists inch closer to fertility breakthrough

In 2008, physicians at the University Hospital in Brussels removed a testicle from a ten-year-old boy and froze tissue samples that had been taken from it. The boy then received chemotherapy in preparation for a blood-stem-cell transplant to treat sickle-cell disease.

More than a decade later, he returned to the hospital as an adult with dreams of having children. Clinicians monitored him for two years and confirmed that he could not produce normal sperm on his own. They then grafted 11 of the tissue fragments — which by then had been frozen for 16 years — either into his remaining testicle or under the skin in his scrotum.

The grafts incubated there for a year, bathed in the hormones and other environmental cues found in an adult man. After that, the tissue fragments were removed and examined.

The team found sperm-producing stem cells and signs of active sperm production in several of the transplants. In a sample from one graft, they found a single mature sperm.

At that point, the researchers stopped their analysis and saved the rest of the graft in the hope that more sperm could be collected from it later and used for in vitro fertilization, says Ellen Goossens, a reproductive biologist at Vrije Universiteit Brussel and a co-author of the preprint.

Future father?
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough restoring fertility in a man through transplantation of testicular tissue frozen before chemotherapy in childhood. This achievement offers hope to many childhood cancer survivors facing infertility, with broad implications for fertility treatments worldwide. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful positive impact on health and quality of life.
articles.personality_title: Frozen testicle tissue restores sperm production after 16 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some boys who need chemotherapy risk losing their ability to have children because the treatment can harm their testicles. To protect their future fertility, doctors sometimes take a small piece of testicle tissue and freeze it before treatment.

**What happened** – A man who had testicle tissue removed and frozen when he was 10 years old had it transplanted back into his body 16 years later. After a year, doctors found that the tissue was making sperm again. This tissue had been frozen before he received chemotherapy for a blood disease.

**Impact** – This is the first time doctors have shown that frozen testicle tissue taken from a child and transplanted back as an adult can start producing sperm. It means boys who face treatments that might make them infertile could still have a chance to father children later.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to collect more sperm from the transplanted tissue to help the man have children through in vitro fertilization (IVF). They also want to test this method with more patients to make it a standard option for preserving fertility.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Freezing and later transplanting testicle tissue can restore sperm production in men treated with chemotherapy as children, opening new paths for fertility preservation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Du tissu testiculaire congelé restaure la production de spermatozoïdes après 16 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certains garçons qui doivent suivre une chimiothérapie risquent de perdre leur capacité à avoir des enfants, car le traitement peut endommager leurs testicules. Pour protéger leur fertilité future, les médecins prélèvent parfois un petit morceau de tissu testiculaire et le congèlent avant le traitement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un homme à qui l'on avait retiré et congelé du tissu testiculaire à l'âge de 10 ans l'a vu transplanté dans son corps 16 ans plus tard. Après un an, les médecins ont constaté que le tissu produisait à nouveau des spermatozoïdes. Ce tissu avait été congelé avant qu'il ne reçoive une chimiothérapie pour une maladie du sang.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois que des médecins montrent que du tissu testiculaire congelé prélevé chez un enfant et transplanté à l'âge adulte peut recommencer à produire des spermatozoïdes. Cela signifie que les garçons qui suivent des traitements risquant de les rendre infertiles pourraient encore avoir une chance de devenir pères plus tard.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent récupérer davantage de spermatozoïdes à partir du tissu transplanté pour aider l'homme à avoir des enfants grâce à la fécondation in vitro (FIV). Ils veulent aussi tester cette méthode sur plus de patients pour en faire une option standard de préservation de la fertilité.

**Une phrase clé** – La congélation et la transplantation ultérieure de tissu testiculaire peuvent restaurer la production de spermatozoïdes chez les hommes traités par chimiothérapie enfants, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour préserver la fertilité.
articles.personality_title_es: Tejido testicular congelado restaura la producción de esperma después de 16 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunos niños que necesitan quimioterapia corren el riesgo de perder la capacidad para tener hijos porque el tratamiento puede dañar sus testículos. Para proteger su fertilidad futura, los médicos a veces extraen un pequeño trozo de tejido testicular y lo congelan antes del tratamiento.

**Qué pasó** – Un hombre a quien le habían extraído y congelado tejido testicular cuando tenía 10 años recibió una nueva implantación 16 años después. Tras un año, los médicos descubrieron que el tejido volvía a producir esperma. Este tejido se congeló antes de que él recibiera quimioterapia para una enfermedad de la sangre.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los médicos demuestran que el tejido testicular congelado tomado de un niño y trasplantado en la edad adulta puede comenzar a producir esperma. Esto significa que los niños que enfrentan tratamientos que podrían causar infertilidad aún podrían tener la oportunidad de ser padres después.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan recoger más esperma del tejido trasplantado para ayudar al hombre a tener hijos mediante fertilización in vitro (FIV). También quieren probar este método con más pacientes para convertirlo en una opción estándar para preservar la fertilidad.

**Una frase clave** – Congelar y luego trasplantar tejido testicular puede restaurar la producción de esperma en hombres tratados con quimioterapia en la infancia, abriendo nuevas opciones para preservar la fertilidad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate, glowing orange seminiferous tubule structure filled with vibrant blue developing sperm cells inside, gently cradled within a translucent, protective hand-shaped form symbolizing care and restoration.

articles.title: CRISPR gets a power boost from AI-designed ‘molecular scissors’
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 16 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02217-wResearchers used artificial intelligence to design functional CRISPR enzymes not seen in nature.
articles.content:
An enzyme cuts DNA (artist’s impression). Synthetic protein-cleaving enzymes were designed quickly with the help of artificial intelligence.Credit: Artur Plawgo/Science Photo Library

Scientists have harnessed artificial-intelligence models to create synthetic CRISPR proteins that edit the genome more efficiently than their naturally occurring counterparts. Such synthetic CRISPR systems could one day power discoveries in fields from medicine to agriculture.

The results1 were published on 16 July in Science.

“Much like CRISPR democratized the ability to edit DNA at will, AI-based protein design promises to allow anyone to create totally novel properties in the protein space,” says Soeren Lienkamp, a molecular biologist at the University of Zurich in Switzerland who was not involved in the research. He adds that the paper “marries two transformative fields”: AI-guided design and enzymes called RNA-guided nucleases, which can cut DNA and RNA strands.

Snip, snip

These nucleases form the backbone of the gene-editing system known as CRISPR, which uses a ‘guide RNA’ to direct the nuclease to a target DNA sequence. The nuclease then acts like molecular scissors and snips out the targeted material, enabling scientists to edit, delete or add genetic information. CRISPR systems are based on the machinery that bacteria use to defend themselves against viruses. The most common CRISPR nucleases, such as Cas9 and Cas12, are co-opted from bacteria.

As powerful a tool as gene editing is, the process is complex. Nucleases must complete a carefully orchestrated series of steps, making it difficult to move beyond what evolution has already produced, says Jennifer Doudna, a biochemist at the University of California, Berkeley, and lead author of the paper, who shared the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for her work on CRISPR systems. “Once you start tweaking things, you realize pretty quickly that while you can make changes, they ultimately produce something that isn’t functional.”

Ex-Meta scientists debut gigantic AI protein design model

AI tools offer the opportunity to supercharge the process of identifying promising candidates for new, functional nucleases. Rather than performing hundreds or even thousands of exploratory experiments, researchers could, in theory, ask machine learning to do it for them. To test this idea, Doudna and her collaborators focused on creating synthetic versions of a group of tiny nucleases called TnpBs, which are evolutionary precursors to the commonly used Cas12. The scientists wanted to know how far they could alter the proteins’ sequences while retaining the proteins’ ability to edit genes.

For a protein to function, it often needs to assume a certain shape, or conformation. The team began by providing an AI model with the final conformation of a type of TnpB and asking it to reverse-engineer changes to the underlying DNA templates that would nevertheless maintain the protein’s final shape. This approach produced thousands of potential changes, but said nothing about whether the resulting protein would be active.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where AI is used to design synthetic CRISPR enzymes that outperform natural ones, potentially revolutionizing gene editing. This advancement has broad implications for medicine and agriculture, offering a powerful tool to improve health outcomes and food security. The article provides detailed context on the technology, its development, and its potential impact, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: AI designs powerful new CRISPR enzymes to improve gene editing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – CRISPR is a tool that allows scientists to edit DNA by cutting it at specific spots. This tool uses proteins called nucleases, which act like tiny scissors guided by RNA to make precise cuts. These proteins originally come from bacteria.

**What happened** – Scientists used artificial intelligence (AI) to design new versions of CRISPR enzymes that do not exist in nature. These synthetic enzymes, based on a small group called TnpBs, were created by teaching AI to change their DNA while keeping the protein’s important shape. The new enzymes were found to work better than natural ones for editing genes. The research was published on July 16, 2026, in the journal Science.

**Impact** – This is important because gene editing is usually limited by what nature has already made. Using AI to design new enzymes means scientists can create tools that work faster or more precisely. This could help in many areas, like developing better medicines or improving crops for farming, by making gene editing easier and more effective.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue testing these AI-designed enzymes in different experiments to understand their full potential. They might also use AI to design other types of proteins for new uses. This approach could speed up discoveries and lead to new technologies in biology and medicine.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have used AI to create new CRISPR enzymes that work better than natural ones, opening new possibilities for gene editing in health and agriculture.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA conçoit de nouvelles enzymes CRISPR plus efficaces pour améliorer l’édition génétique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le CRISPR est un outil qui permet aux scientifiques de modifier l’ADN en le coupant à des endroits précis. Cet outil utilise des protéines appelées nucléases, qui agissent comme de petites ciseaux guidés par l’ARN pour faire des coupures précises. Ces protéines viennent à l’origine de bactéries.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour concevoir de nouvelles versions d’enzymes CRISPR qui n’existent pas dans la nature. Ces enzymes synthétiques, basées sur un petit groupe appelé TnpB, ont été créées en apprenant à l’IA à modifier leur ADN tout en gardant la forme importante de la protéine. Ces nouvelles enzymes fonctionnent mieux que celles naturelles pour modifier les gènes. La recherche a été publiée le 16 juillet 2026 dans la revue Science.

**Impact** – C’est important parce que l’édition génétique est souvent limitée par ce que la nature a déjà créé. Utiliser l’IA pour concevoir de nouvelles enzymes signifie que les scientifiques peuvent créer des outils qui fonctionnent plus rapidement ou plus précisément. Cela pourrait aider dans de nombreux domaines, comme le développement de meilleurs médicaments ou l’amélioration des cultures agricoles, en rendant l’édition génétique plus facile et efficace.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à tester ces enzymes conçues par IA dans différentes expériences pour comprendre tout leur potentiel. Ils pourraient aussi utiliser l’IA pour concevoir d’autres types de protéines pour de nouvelles utilisations. Cette méthode pourrait accélérer les découvertes et mener à de nouvelles technologies en biologie et en médecine.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé l’IA pour créer de nouvelles enzymes CRISPR plus efficaces que celles naturelles, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour l’édition génétique en santé et en agriculture.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA diseña nuevas enzimas CRISPR más potentes para mejorar la edición genética
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – CRISPR es una herramienta que permite a los científicos editar el ADN cortándolo en lugares específicos. Esta herramienta usa proteínas llamadas nucleasas, que actúan como pequeñas tijeras guiadas por ARN para hacer cortes precisos. Estas proteínas provienen originalmente de bacterias.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos utilizaron inteligencia artificial (IA) para diseñar nuevas versiones de enzimas CRISPR que no existen en la naturaleza. Estas enzimas sintéticas, basadas en un grupo pequeño llamado TnpBs, fueron creadas enseñando a la IA a cambiar su ADN mientras mantenía la forma importante de la proteína. Se encontró que estas nuevas enzimas funcionan mejor que las naturales para editar genes. La investigación se publicó el 16 de julio de 2026 en la revista Science.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la edición genética suele estar limitada por lo que la naturaleza ya ha producido. Usar IA para diseñar nuevas enzimas significa que los científicos pueden crear herramientas que funcionan más rápido o con más precisión. Esto podría ayudar en áreas como el desarrollo de mejores medicinas o la mejora de cultivos agrícolas, haciendo la edición genética más fácil y efectiva.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán probando estas enzimas diseñadas por IA en diferentes experimentos para entender todo su potencial. También podrían usar IA para diseñar otros tipos de proteínas para nuevos usos. Este enfoque podría acelerar los descubrimientos y llevar a nuevas tecnologías en biología y medicina.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos usaron IA para crear nuevas enzimas CRISPR que funcionan mejor que las naturales, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para la edición genética en salud y agricultura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of intricately shaped molecular scissors delicately cutting a glowing, spiraling DNA strand, with softly glowing, abstract AI neural network patterns subtly woven into the background to symbolize artificial intelligence guiding the design.

articles.title: A cheap, homegrown catnip lotion could replace DEET for millions of Ugandans
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM A lotion made from catnip oil matched DEET in field trials in Uganda, according to research presented at the Society for Experimental Biology conference in Florence last week. Catnip (Nepeta cataria), a common herb in the mint family, contains nepetalactone, the compound that makes cats go sideways. It also […]
The post A cheap, homegrown catnip lotion could replace DEET for millions of Ugandans first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

A lotion made from catnip oil matched DEET in field trials in Uganda, according to research presented at the Society for Experimental Biology conference in Florence last week.

Catnip (Nepeta cataria), a common herb in the mint family, contains nepetalactone, the compound that makes cats go sideways. It also repels insects, which has been known for years. Nobody had turned it into a product. That’s what this research is trying to change.

What the trial found

Researchers from Cardiff University, working with partners in Uganda, tested four groups: volunteers wearing DEET lotion (15 percent, the most available formulation in Uganda), a six percent catnip oil lotion, a two percent catnip oil lotion, and a placebo. They counted mosquito landings on volunteers’ legs over an evening in eastern Uganda.

“We found that a six percent catnip oil was just as effective as DEET, and the two percent catnip oil was only marginally less effective than that,” said Dr. Simon Scofield, a senior lecturer at Cardiff University who led the research.

Lab tests confirmed catnip oil could work as a repellent. The field trial was the more challenging test.

The malaria context

Malaria infects about 282 million people a year. It killed 610,000 in 2024, most of them young children in sub-Saharan Africa. Insecticide resistance is rising, and so is resistance to frontline treatment drugs.

DEET is the most widely used repellent in the world, but most of the people who need it most can’t afford it. “DEET is out of the price bracket for most rural Ugandan subsistence farmers, so buying commercially available mosquito repellents is just not practicable,” Scofield said. Travelers from the US and Europe heading to malaria-endemic regions are typically advised to use formulations of at least 50 percent DEET. The product tested against catnip in Uganda was 15 percent. Catnip grows locally. That’s not incidental to the research; it’s the driving motivation for it.

Making it locally

The team also tested whether the lotion could be produced by a community enterprise. So far it’s been distributed free under grant funding. The next step is scaling production and selling it so the supply chain can survive without grants.

“Once we know that we can sell and distribute the repellent at a low cost, that should generate a self-sustaining system where the money is flowing back to everybody at each stage in the development,” Scofield said.

One real limitation

Swai Kyeba, a research entomologist at the Ifakara Health Institute in Tanzania who wasn’t involved in the study, welcomed the direction. “New vector-control tools are necessary in the fight against malaria, especially those that are cheap and locally produced,” he said.

His reservation is one that applies to all topical repellents: people have to actually use them. “A challenge with topical repellents is low compliance because they require regular application,” Kyeba said. “This is why they remain a complementary tool in the fight against malaria.”

That’s true of DEET too. No repellent works if it stays in the cabinet. A cheap, locally made lotion narrows that gap. It doesn’t close it.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant health innovation: a cheap, locally produced catnip oil lotion that matches the effectiveness of DEET in repelling mosquitoes, potentially benefiting millions in malaria-endemic Uganda. This has broad public health implications by providing an affordable, accessible alternative to expensive repellents, addressing a major disease burden with detailed research and field trial evidence.
articles.personality_title: Catnip lotion offers affordable mosquito protection for Ugandans
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Malaria is a serious disease in Uganda, infecting millions and killing many, especially children. Mosquito repellents like DEET help protect people but are often too expensive for many Ugandans. Researchers have been looking for cheaper, local alternatives.

**What happened** – Scientists from Cardiff University and partners in Uganda tested a lotion made from catnip oil, a local herb known to repel insects. They compared it to DEET lotion in a field trial by counting mosquito landings on volunteers’ legs. The lotion with 6% catnip oil worked just as well as the 15% DEET lotion, while 2% catnip was nearly as effective.

**Impact** – This is important because catnip grows locally and is cheaper to produce than DEET products. If made and sold in Uganda, this lotion could give many people better access to protection against malaria-carrying mosquitoes. It could improve health by reducing mosquito bites without high costs.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to start producing the lotion in local community businesses. Right now, it has been given away for free with grants. The goal is to sell it at low prices so the project can continue without outside funding, helping local economies and keeping supplies steady.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A lotion made from locally grown catnip oil can protect people from mosquitoes as well as DEET, offering a cheaper, homegrown option for malaria prevention in Uganda.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une lotion à la cataire offre une protection abordable contre les moustiques en Ouganda
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le paludisme est une maladie grave en Ouganda, infectant des millions de personnes et tuant beaucoup d’enfants. Les répulsifs comme le DEET protègent, mais sont souvent trop chers pour beaucoup d’Ougandais. Les chercheurs cherchent des alternatives locales moins coûteuses.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Cardiff et leurs partenaires ougandais ont testé une lotion à base d’huile de cataire, une plante locale connue pour repousser les insectes. Ils ont comparé cette lotion au DEET en comptant les piqûres de moustiques sur les jambes des volontaires. La lotion avec 6% d’huile de cataire était aussi efficace que celle avec 15% de DEET, et 2% d’huile était presque aussi bonne.

**Impact** – C’est important car la cataire pousse localement et coûte moins cher à produire que le DEET. Si la lotion est fabriquée et vendue en Ouganda, elle pourrait permettre à beaucoup plus de personnes de se protéger des moustiques qui transmettent le paludisme, sans dépenser beaucoup.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent commencer à produire la lotion dans des entreprises communautaires locales. Jusqu’à présent, elle a été distribuée gratuitement grâce à des subventions. L’objectif est de la vendre à bas prix pour que le projet puisse continuer sans financement extérieur, en aidant l’économie locale.

**Phrase clé** – Une lotion à base d’huile de cataire locale peut protéger aussi bien que le DEET, offrant une option moins chère et locale pour prévenir le paludisme en Ouganda.
articles.personality_title_es: Loción de hierba gatera ofrece protección económica contra mosquitos en Uganda
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La malaria es una enfermedad grave en Uganda, que infecta a millones y causa muchas muertes, especialmente en niños. Los repelentes como el DEET ayudan a proteger, pero suelen ser muy caros para muchos ugandeses. Los investigadores buscan alternativas locales más baratas.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Cardiff y socios en Uganda probaron una loción hecha con aceite de hierba gatera, una planta local que repele insectos. Compararon esta loción con DEET en un ensayo de campo contando las picaduras de mosquitos en las piernas de voluntarios. La loción con 6% de aceite de hierba gatera fue tan efectiva como la de 15% DEET, y la de 2% casi igual.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la hierba gatera crece localmente y es más barata de producir que los productos con DEET. Si se fabrica y vende en Uganda, esta loción podría ayudar a muchas personas a protegerse de los mosquitos transmisores de malaria sin gastar mucho.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren comenzar a producir la loción en negocios comunitarios locales. Hasta ahora se ha distribuido gratis con fondos de subvenciones. La meta es venderla a bajo costo para que el proyecto continúe sin financiamiento externo y apoye la economía local.

**Frase clave** – Una loción hecha con aceite local de hierba gatera puede proteger contra los mosquitos tan bien como el DEET, ofreciendo una opción más barata y local para prevenir la malaria en Uganda.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of vibrant catnip plants gently swaying beside a rustic clay pot filled with creamy lotion, set against a soft, earthy Ugandan landscape at dusk, with delicate mosquito silhouettes hovering nearby but kept at bay by a subtle glowing aura emanating from the lotion.
